title: Podman should detach from non-interactive mode containers
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug


**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
--> 

Unable to detach from a container using the default keys ctrl-p,ctrl-q or by setting different keys in the /etc/containers/container.conf file.  

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Attach to a container "podman attach containername" 

2. press detach key sequence "ctrl-p, ctrl-q" 
 

**Describe the results you received:**
Nothing happens, the cursor indicates its still in the container with a blank output or whatever is expected of that container. The terminal has to be exited to continue working as no escape keys will exit the container

**Describe the results you expected:**
The terminal should exit the pod and return to the local system bash prompt.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
n/a
**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 2.0.2

```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman/unknown,now 2.0.2~2 amd64 [installed]
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
physical, I'm also running this on a Centos 8 machine which is a vm in vsphere 6.5 with the same issue.

comments.body: @QiWang19 PTAL

@Keith-Nowosielski-SJSU  I failed to reproduce this detach key issue with 2.0.2 or the current upstream branch. ctrl-p, ctrl-q will only work when you have started your container with interactive mode (-it). Maybe you have changed the `detach_keys` in containers.conf to other keys?

Qi, Yes I did discover they work with interactive mode. Is there just no
way to detach in non-interactive mode?

Thanks,

Keith


On Fri, Jul 24, 2020 at 1:22 PM Qi Wang <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> @Keith-Nowosielski-SJSU <https://github.com/Keith-Nowosielski-SJSU> I
> failed to reproduce this detach key issue with 2.0.2 or the current
> upstream branch. ctrl-p, ctrl-q will only work when you have started your
> container with interactive mode (-it). Maybe you have changed the
> detach_keys in containers.conf to other keys?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/6990#issuecomment-663718341>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AQJEB5FHSMMVVX4AZOMJXR3R5HURVANCNFSM4O237ACA>
> .
>
Do you mean this, run with -d,  `-d, --detach                                   Run container in background and print container ID`?

With `--sig-proxy=false` set, you could control-c Podman and kill just it, not the container - I don't think we can do that by default, though, even for non-interactive containers.

I suppose we could also add a client-side loop for non-interactive Podman that listens for the detach keys and stops Podman if they're received?

podman attach container_name <- this is what I use, it gives the output
from the pod if there is any similar to a telnet session into a network
device. The podman documentation reads like the detach keys should work to
exit this mode and return to the local system. Otherwise all I can do is
close the terminal and start a new one.


Keith


On Fri, Jul 24, 2020 at 1:34 PM Qi Wang <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> and print container ID?

Yes this should allow you to detach.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@QiWang19 Have you been able to look into @mheon suggestion?
@QiWang19 I think we need to raise the importance of this issue.
@Keith-Nowosielski-SJSU @mheon Do we still have this issue?
This has not been fixed.
Wish I could give you an answer. My podman installation is currently giving me [this issue](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/7396) for all commands so far as I can see.
Just confirmed this is still an issue in podman 2.1.1
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon could you spend some time on this one?
I'll see what I can do this week

Looking more into this today - I'm starting to lean towards this not being possible. We can detect the normal detech keys (Ctrl-p, Ctrl-Q) because the terminal is placed in raw mode as part of `-t` and we get raw bytes off the terminal. Without `-t` we are not in raw mode (and I don't believe we can be - the point of running Podman without `-t` is to ensure that output is plaintext and can be easily used with pipes, shell redirection, etc and raw mode would prevent that) and as such I can't tell if the control key is depressed - it's being handled at a higher level and we have no idea about it. I tested with the latest Docker, and `docker run -i` also displays this behavior - detach keys are ignored.

I'm going to close this as a WONTFIX - we can't do this without breaking the main intent of Podman without `-t`.

Can the documentation then be updated to reflect this? I was still
relatively new to podman when I happened across this and it was really
frustrating because the documentation spells this out as the proper
functionality. The first thing I would do is run a plain jane attach and
hit this problem.

http://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-attach.1.html

Thanks,

Keith Nowosielski

Information Technology Systems Specialist

Computing Services Department

San Jose State University


On Tue, Mar 9, 2021 at 1:29 PM Matthew Heon <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> Looking more into this today - I'm starting to lean towards this not being
> possible. We can detect the normal detech keys (Ctrl-p, Ctrl-Q) because the
> terminal is placed in raw mode as part of -t and we get raw bytes off the
> terminal. Without -t we are not in raw mode (and I don't believe we can
> be - the point of running Podman without -t is to ensure that output is
> plaintext and can be easily used with pipes, shell redirection, etc and raw
> mode would prevent that) and as such I can't tell if the control key is
> depressed - it's being handled at a higher level and we have no idea about
> it. I tested with the latest Docker, and docker run -i also displays this
> behavior - detach keys are ignored.
>
> I'm going to close this as a WONTFIX - we can't do this without breaking
> the main intent of Podman without -t.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/6990#issuecomment-794479769>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AQJEB5H2VFS4AXCN26264PLTC2AMDANCNFSM4O237ACA>
> .
>
Interested in opening a PR with documentation that would have helped you?

> Without `-t` we are not in raw mode (and I don't believe we can be - the point of running Podman without `-t` is to ensure that output is plaintext and can be easily used with pipes, shell redirection, etc and raw mode would prevent that) and as such I can't tell if the control key is depressed - it's being handled at a higher level and we have no idea about it.

In text mode (non -t) how is "Ctrl-C" handled (in that it can terminate a process)?
It seems to be passed to the process but because we are not in '-t' raw mode, podman doesn't have access to "capture" those characters?
Can we not capture them at all or can we just not do the "wait to see if the sequence matches detatch, and if not, send them" to the process?

I ran into this several times in the past and recently as well. I agree the documentation could be better. The best description I have seen so far is https://stackoverflow.com/a/41099052

In normal mode, I believe it's the shell that's handling all of that - so the shell gets a Ctrl-C, and subsequently sends the foregrounded process a signal to terminate. We have absolutely no indication that the control key is being pressed, we're just getting a stream of unicode characters that are the final result of whatever keys are being pressed. Some key cominations don't actually produce output, but rather side effects. It's similar to how Ctrl-D isn't actually sending an EOF character, but instead is closing the STDIN stream.

I understand a bit more after playing around. The trouble is that there is no way to exit the "attach" without forwarding any signal to the underlying process. Maybe this is desirable? I haven't seen the use case for it yet, but I find it odd that *omitting* `-t` consistently was getting me in trouble.

Possible options:
* Do we have any options to use a POSIX signal which can detach without forwarding to the underlying process?
* warning (with confirmation?) when using `attach` or `-i` on a container in raw mode

A `kill -9` on the Podman process performing the attach is guaranteed to get rid of it, but not the container. Other signals unfortunately won't work (we default to using `--sig-proxy`, which proxies (almost) all signals that can be caught into the container; we need a signal that cannot be caught to successfully terminate Podman itself, hence SIGKILL).
